text: Objection
Sustain
Overrule
Inadmissible
Strike
Relevance
Speculation
Foundation
Sidebar
Argumentative
Prejudicial
Interject
If counsel continues, I will object.
If the question calls for speculation, the judge will sustain it.
If the court allowed that, it would prejudice the jury.
If I were the prosecutor, I would withdraw the question.
Counsel, you are on your feet. What is your objection?
Objection, Your Honor. The question calls for speculation. If the witness answers, the jury will hear a guess, not a fact.
Sustained. Rephrase your question. And please do not interject again before I rule.
Thank you. I also move to strike the previous answer; it had no foundation.
On what ground is it inadmissible?
Relevance, Your Honor. If that statement stays on the record, it would be prejudicial to my client.
Approach. We will settle this at a sidebar, away from the jury.
Thank you, Your Honor. If the court overrules me, I will note it for appeal.

Before we begin, let me explain to the new attorneys how objections work in this court. An objection is your tool to protect the record, but timing is everything. If a question calls for speculation, you must object before the witness answers. If you wait, the harm is done, and I can only strike the answer from the record. When you object, give me one ground: relevance, foundation, hearsay, or that the question is argumentative. If the objection is valid, I will sustain it; if it is not, I will overrule it and the witness will answer. If the matter is sensitive, ask for a sidebar and we will discuss it away from the jury. Above all, do not interject while a colleague is speaking. Rise, say objection, and wait for me. If you would interrupt with authority, you must first interrupt with respect.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. In court today, the defense raised a series of well-timed objections that shaped the trial. When the prosecutor asked a question with no foundation, counsel rose at once and the judge sustained the objection. Later, an answer that was clearly prejudicial was struck from the record. If the defense had stayed silent, that statement would have reached the jury. At one point the lawyers requested a sidebar, and after a brief discussion the disputed evidence was ruled inadmissible. Reporters were told that if the trial continues at this pace, a verdict could come by Friday. We will follow the case as it develops.
